title: podman build fails if dnf inside Dockerfile
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

When running podman build, the process fails as the container OS can't resolve a repository url
The host machine can resolve the url with no problem

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Run podman build with a Dockerfile that contains a dnf command

2. the build fails with:
```
Errors during downloading metadata for repository '[...]':
  - Curl error (28): Timeout was reached for [...]repomd.xml [Resolving timed out after 30000 milliseconds]
Error: Failed to download metadata for repo '[...]': Cannot download repomd.xml: Cannot download repodata/repomd.xml: All mirrors were tried
Error: error building at STEP "RUN dnf upgrade -y": error while running runtime: exit status 1
```

**Describe the results you expected:**
build process with dnf command should finish with no errors

**Output of `podman version`:**
```
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.0.2
API Version:  4.0.2
Go Version:   go1.17.5

Built:      Mon Mar 14 16:13:09 2022
OS/Arch:    linux/amd64
```

comments.body:
Hi @elfosardo I can't see your entire container file or setup so would be hard to tell. But could you try a different base image to test if networking is working fine, maybe `ubuntu:latest` and do `apt-get update` inside container. 

I think most likely its issue with your container's `dnf` config, if networking is working fine for other base images.

@flouthoc while doing the test, some more info, I'm using ubi8 as base image -> https://catalog.redhat.com/software/containers/ubi8/ubi/5c359854d70cc534b3a3784e?container-tabs=overview

I'm not modifying any dnf related configuration or any other part, just adding some internal repositories and running dnf upgrade

@flouthoc I was having issues pulling the ubuntu image so I tried with a simple centos image, my Dockerfile contains only:
```
FROM centos:stream8
RUN dnf upgrade -y
```
same result
```
STEP 2/2: RUN dnf upgrade -y
CentOS Stream 8 - AppStream                     0.0  B/s |   0  B     06:24    
Errors during downloading metadata for repository 'appstream':
  - Curl error (28): Timeout was reached for http://mirrorlist.centos.org/?release=8-stream&arch=x86_64&repo=AppStream&infra=stock [Resolving timed out after 30000 milliseconds]
Error: Failed to download metadata for repo 'appstream': Cannot prepare internal mirrorlist: Curl error (28): Timeout was reached for http://mirrorlist.centos.org/?release=8-stream&arch=x86_64&repo=AppStream&infra=stock [Resolving timed out after 30000 milliseconds]
Error: error building at STEP "RUN dnf upgrade -y": error while running runtime: exit status 1
```

@elfosardo Could you try with `podman build --network=private -t test .`, although it seems like there is a issue with your network cause on your local its working fine cause mirrorlist is cached but inside container is failing while getting the mirror list.
I would think that `--network private` is already the default so I would try with `--network host` first.

@flouthoc @Luap99 thanks, using `--network host` seems to work
not sure what the root cause is though
@elfosardo Could you try without `--network host` as well now ? I am assuming it could have been something with your network.
@flouthoc just did another test, removing `--network host` brings back the issue and the build fails because it can't resolve the repo url
@elfosardo Does any network call work at all when you remove `--network host`, could you try `RUN curl google.com` or something similar ?
@flouthoc I was actually doing some tests with pure curl, and from what I can see no host can be resolved during build without `--network host` option
@elfosardo Does curl call work inside a running container ? Could you run this command please `podman run -it --rm centos:stream8 curl google.com`

that works: ``` podman run -it --rm centos:stream8 curl google.com 301 Moved

301 Moved

The document has moved here. ```
I have the same issue, but for me the situation is even more confusing: `dnf` works in `podman build`, but not in `sudo podman build` (using the same source).
@elfosardo Can you provide the output of `ip link` on the host. I susspect there could be a mtu mismatch here.

My `ip link` if it helps:
```
$ ip link
1: lo: <LOOPBACK,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65536 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN mode DEFAULT group default qlen 1000
    link/loopback 00:00:00:00:00:00 brd 00:00:00:00:00:00

    link/ether cc:c5:e5:62:4d:24 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
4: virbr0: <NO-CARRIER,BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP> mtu 1500 qdisc noqueue state DOWN mode DEFAULT group default qlen 1000
    link/ether 52:54:00:29:01:8b brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
8: cni-podman0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc noqueue state UP mode DEFAULT group default qlen 1000
    link/ether 9a:b8:7e:05:e5:0f brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
158: ostestpr: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN mode DEFAULT group default qlen 1000
    link/ether ce:f6:87:ff:15:3f brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
159: ostestbm: <NO-CARRIER,BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP> mtu 1500 qdisc noqueue state DOWN mode DEFAULT group default qlen 1000
    link/ether 52:54:00:34:f7:27 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
163: veth005b9cc1@if2: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc noqueue master cni-podman0 state UP mode DEFAULT group default 
    link/ether d6:37:ac:ae:c4:29 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff link-netnsid 0
```

The fun continues:
```
$ sudo podman run -it --rm centos:stream8 curl -I google.com
curl: (6) Could not resolve host: google.com

$ podman run -it --rm centos:stream8 curl -I google.com
HTTP/1.1 301 Moved Permanently
Location: http://www.google.com/
Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8
Date: Wed, 23 Mar 2022 09:24:44 GMT
Expires: Fri, 22 Apr 2022 09:24:44 GMT
Cache-Control: public, max-age=2592000
Server: gws
Content-Length: 219
X-XSS-Protection: 0
X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN
```
I don't see this issue with Podman 3.4.4 on my Fedora 35. Could it be a regression in 4.0.x? Of course, we cannot rule out our environments as well.

@dtantsur You might have a different issue. Do you use cni or netavark? Plese check in podman info for network backend.

@elfosardo Do you run as root or rootless?

Oookay, interesting (sorry for spam - posting as I discover things). I see the same issue with podman 3.4.1-dev on my outdated Stream 8 machine but NOT in a Stream8 VM inside that machine. Both are completely different environments from the first machine, so I think we can rule out both podman 4.0 and most of potential environmental issues.
@flouthoc same as @dtantsur , sudo doesn't work, without sudo it works

Podman infos:
- From the first broken machine: https://pastebin.com/NnerLJJw
- From the second broken machine: https://pastebin.com/n2m08Ac4
- From the working VM: https://pastebin.com/ir6ieRsA

The first machine has `networkBackend: cni`, the other two do not list a networkBackend.

I suspect there is something wrong with the iptables rules.
Can you paste the output `iptables -nvL -t nat` and `iptables -nvL`
networkBackend is new with 4.0 older versions always use cni

my `ip link` output is a bit longer, but essentially similar to @dtantsur 
btw I'm on CentOS Stream 9 at the moment

@Luap99 that works, and yes the issue is the dns, I think I mentioned that in the description of the issue

`Resolving timed out after 30000 milliseconds` can mean anything, it doesn't have to be related to dns.

Can you paste the output of `podman run -it --rm --network bridge centos:stream8 cat /etc/resolv.conf`

@Luap99 output of `cat /etc/resolv.conf`

```
search dev1.kni.lab.eng.bos.redhat.com dev2.kni.lab.eng.bos.redhat.com
nameserver 8.8.8.8
nameserver 8.8.4.4

```
Is 8.8.8.8 blocked by your firewall? Try `nslookup google.com 8.8.8.8` from the host. What is in your `/etc/resolv.conf` on the host.

I seem to vaguely recall that either our internal network blocks 8.8.8.8 or (more likely) 8.8.8.8 blocks our internal network. This may be the clue indeed:
```
$ nslookup google.com 8.8.8.8
;; connection timed out; no servers could be reached
```
but then I don't understand the difference between `sudo` and no `sudo`.

> I seem to vaguely recall that either our internal network blocks 8.8.8.8 or (more likely) 8.8.8.8 blocks our internal network. This may be the clue indeed:
> 
> ```
> $ nslookup google.com 8.8.8.8
> ;; connection timed out; no servers could be reached
> ```
> 
> but then I don't understand the difference between `sudo` and no `sudo`.

I confirm it's blocked, and also puzzled by the sudo-no-sudo difference

So the difference can be explained easily. Rootless uses slirp4netns which acts as userspace proxy. It allows us to use the system resolver because it is just another process in the host namespace.

If you run as root it will route traffic normally with firewall rules. I think your resolver contains only localhost addresses they cannot be reached from the container.

It would be very helpful if you could provide the /etc/resolv.conf content on your host. We already have a special case for systemd-resolved where we have to read the resolvers from a different files.

@Luap99 this is the content of /etc/resolv.conf in the host
```
# Generated by NetworkManager
search dev1.kni.lab.eng.bos.redhat.com dev2.kni.lab.eng.bos.redhat.com
nameserver 127.0.0.1
options edns0 trust-ad
```

Ok in this case our behaviour is correct and you have configuration problem. Because it is not possible to reach 127.0.0.1 from the container we remove it from /etc/resolv.conf. Now you have no dns servers left so we will add the default google servers.

Possible solutions:
* let your dns server run on the eth0 ip and use this in /etc/resolv.conf
* configure extra nameservers in /etc/resolv.conf that are not blocked
* configure the dns servers in containers.conf and use some that are not blocked.

What dns server do you have running on localhost? Is there some way for podman to read the actual upstream resolvers (we already have such a workaround for systemd-resolved)?

@Luap99 thanks for the analysis and the help
The host we use is a development machine with dnsmasq
I think the only viable solutions for our case is to try having extra namservers in /etc/resolv.conf or containers.conf
I'll do some tests, worst case scenario we have the `--network host` option as a workaround

@Luap99 thank you so much!

Note that this behaviour affects all systems that use a local dnsmasq as a resolver, which may be all modern distributions that use NetworkManager (RHEL, Fedora, Ubuntu, ...?). If anyone has 8.8.8.8 blocked, they will see exactly the same problem.

The default for fedora and also some other distros is systemd-resolved (which uses 127.0.0.53 in resolv.conf), for this we have a workaround because it writes the actual upstream resolvers to `/run/systemd/resolve/resolv.conf`. Unless dnsmasq has a similar fixed location there is not much podman can do besides adding default servers such as 8.8.8.8.
@Luap99 it seems to be `/run/NetworkManager/no-stub-resolv.conf` on my Stream 8 system.
Is this discoverable?

I think so, at least on my default fedora 35 install I also have `/run/NetworkManager/no-stub-resolv.conf` even if it uses systemd-resolved. I reopen this, I think we should check `/run/NetworkManager/no-stub-resolv.conf` first before defaulting to google servers.
SGTM
@Luap99 I confirm also on CentOS Stream 8/9

A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I'm experiencing this issue with podman 4.6.1 and a Fedora 38 image.

Using the `--net host` switch with `run`, any `dnf` command succeeds but tools like `ping`, `curl`, `wget` etc. fail with __Temporary failure in name resolution__ when using a DNS name and not an IP address.

For example, `ping cloudflare.net` shows __Temporary failure in name resolution__. Yet `nslookup cloudflare.net` shows two IPs (104.17.156.85 and 104.16.208.90). `ping 104.17.156.85` shows responses immediately.

If I run `ping 104.17.156.85` without the `--net host` switch I don't get responses back.  Perhaps `dnf` has hard-coded IPs it uses if name resolution fails?

I realize that tools like `dig` and `nslookup` resolve names differently than tools like `wget` and `ping`.  The latter uses the C function `getaddrinfo` while the former actually sends DNS requests directly.  Running `getent ahosts cloudflare.net` returns with no output and an exit code of 2.

This points to perhaps the host DNS being misconfigured.  The host is running Fedora Silverblue 38 and therefore using `systemd-resolved`.  Running `getent ahosts cloudflare.net` on the host returns:
```
104.16.208.90   STREAM cloudflare.net
104.16.208.90   DGRAM  
104.16.208.90   RAW    
104.17.156.85   STREAM 
104.17.156.85   DGRAM  
104.17.156.85   RAW    
```

So it seems the host DNS is configured correctly.

The `/etc/resolv.conf` when using the `--net host` switch in the container is:
```
search shadow.arpa
nameserver 127.0.0.53
options edns0 trust-ad
```
I tried clearing this out and setting it only to the following:
```
nameserver 1.1.1.1
```
But I still get the same problem with `getent` and tools like `ping`.

Shall I file a new bug report for this?
